chapters.title: The Day It Changed
chapters.description: At 06:29 on a Saturday morning — Shabbat — Hamas launched the largest attack on Israel in decades. In under twenty minutes, rockets were falling across the Gaza Envelope, Lakhish, and the coastal lowlands. By nightfall, nearly 4,000 individual alerts had been recorded and 1,200 people were dead.

chapters.title: The Gaza Barrages
chapters.description: Even as Israel's ground offensive pushed deeper into Gaza, the rockets kept coming. Lakhish, the Western Negev, and the Gaza Envelope absorbed near-daily barrages. Short-range, familiar trajectories — the grinding soundtrack of a conflict now stripped of all pretence.
chapters.points_note: Approximate positions within recorded alert areas

chapters.title: The Northern Front
chapters.description: The day after October 7, Hezbollah opened a second front from Lebanon. For eleven months the Confrontation Line — Israel's northern border communities — took near-daily anti-tank fire, rockets, and UAVs. 60,000 residents were evacuated. The north went dark.
chapters.points_note: Approximate positions within recorded alert areas

chapters.title: Houthi Long-Range
chapters.description: From 2,000 kilometres away in Yemen, Houthi drones and ballistic missiles began crossing Saudi Arabia and Jordan to reach Israel. Eilat was hit first. Then the range grew — Jerusalem, Tel Aviv, Ben Gurion airport. No country had ever intercepted missiles at this distance, at this scale.
chapters.points_note: Approximate positions within recorded alert areas

chapters.title: Iran's First Direct Strikes
chapters.description: On April 14, 2024, Iran attacked Israel directly for the first time in history — over 300 drones, cruise missiles, and ballistic missiles. A coalition of five nations intercepted almost everything. On October 1, Iran struck again, with ballistic missiles that reached deeper into Israel's heartland.
chapters.points_note: Approximate positions within recorded alert areas

chapters.title: Total War
chapters.description: On 28 February 2026, Iran launched the largest single missile barrage ever recorded — 10,162 alerts in a single day. Every region of Israel was struck simultaneously, from Eilat to the Golan, coast to Jordan Valley. The conflict had crossed a threshold it would not come back from.
chapters.points_note: Approximate positions within recorded alert areas
